payload.text: I read this as a research task for a 60 FPS TypeScript browser shooter - I will gather primary browser documentation and maintained ECS references, then synthesize a concrete architecture recommendation.

I read this as an architecture research deliverable for a 60 FPS TypeScript browser shooter - below is a concrete recommended stack with tradeoffs and cited sources.

# Recommended stack

For a small-team, stylized first/third-person practice-range shooter:

- **Renderer:** **WebGL2**, implemented with **Three.js `WebGLRenderer`** rather than raw WebGL API calls.
- **Simulation architecture:** a **small custom, data-oriented ECS-like world** with numeric entity IDs and typed-array component stores. Use **bitECS** only if entity counts and composition/query complexity outgrow the custom world.
- **Game loop:** `requestAnimationFrame` render loop + **60 Hz fixed simulation step**, accumulator, capped catch-up steps, and render interpolation.
- **Input:** action-state input layer using `KeyboardEvent.code`, Pointer Lock mouse deltas, optional raw/unadjusted movement, and a reset on blur/visibility loss.
- **Collision:** custom lightweight collision: player cylinder/capsule vs static AABBs, sphere/AABB target hit volumes, hitscan raycasts, and swept projectile segments. Broad-phase with a static + dynamic uniform spatial hash.
- **Assets:** procedural low-poly range geometry plus instanced reusable meshes; atlased textures/billboard effects; `glTF` only for authored animated assets; Web Audio `AudioBuffer`s for short SFX.
- **Performance:** design to a 15 ms p95 frame budget, profile CPU, memory, and GPU separately from the first playable build.

This is intentionally not a full engine architecture. A practice range does not justify a physics engine, a large scene framework, or a generalized ECS dependency until measurement shows a need.

---

# 1. Renderer: WebGL2 vs Canvas 2D

## Recommendation: WebGL2

Use a WebGL2 renderer for the shipped shooter. WebGL exposes hardware-accelerated 2D/3D graphics, and WebGL2 adds OpenGL ES 3-era capabilities including vertex array objects, instancing, uniform buffers, 3D textures, and multiple render targets ([MDN WebGL](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL_API)).

For a small team, use **Three.js as the WebGL2 abstraction**, not raw WebGL unless renderer engineering is a core project goal. It provides cameras, depth testing, materials, asset loading, instancing, and GPU resource lifecycle without requiring the team to create shader/program/buffer infrastructure. Three.js is MIT-licensed and had roughly **113.8k GitHub stars** when reviewed on 2026-07-17 ([repository](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js)).

### Why it is the right default

A first- or third-person shooter naturally needs:

- Perspective projection and a depth buffer.
- Occlusion without manual painter sorting.
- Many repeated targets, props, particles, decals, and tracers.
- GPU-side transforms through instancing.
- Simple stylized lighting, fog, outlines, and post effects.
- A clean growth path from a basic range to denser scenes.

Use low-poly geometry, flat/lambert-like materials, one directional light plus ambient/hemisphere light, carefully limited shadows, and instancing for repeated props and targets. Do not start with expensive screen-space effects or dynamic shadow maps for every light.

MDN recommends batching draw calls and texture atlases: fewer, larger draw calls generally perform better, and atlases reduce texture changes that split batches ([WebGL best practices](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL_API/WebGL_best_practices#batch_draw_calls)).

## When Canvas 2D is appropriate

Canvas 2D is reasonable only when the game is deliberately constrained to **2.5D**:

- Wolfenstein-style raycast corridors or a top-down shooter.
- Fixed low internal resolution, for example 320x180 or 480x270, scaled up.
- A small number of sprites, particles, and world objects.
- No true 3D mesh animation, depth-buffered geometry, dynamic lights, or high-DPI fill-rate requirements.
- A visual direction that benefits from software-like rendering or pixel art.

Canvas 2D can be very good for UI, map overlays, a low-spec fallback, and tightly bounded 2.5D games. It is not the right primary renderer for a general first/third-person 3D shooter because perspective projection, z ordering, visibility, and per-object transforms become CPU-side responsibilities.

Canvas optimization still requires pre-rendering repeated content to an offscreen canvas and minimizing repeated drawing work ([MDN Canvas optimization](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Canvas_API/Tutorial/Optimizing_canvas)). `OffscreenCanvas` can move canvas work to a worker, but that is an optimization to apply after profiling, not an initial architectural requirement ([MDN OffscreenCanvas](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/OffscreenCanvas)).

| Choice | Strengths | Costs | Use it when |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WebGL2** | Native 3D pipeline, depth, GPU instancing, shaders, scalable particles/meshes | GPU resource management, shader/material complexity, context-loss handling | Default for this shooter |
| **Canvas 2D** | Very fast iteration, simple API, excellent for HUD and bounded 2D | No programmable 3D pipeline; 3D is CPU projection/sorting; scaling ceiling is lower | Purposefully limited 2.5D/pixel-art game |

Feature-detect the context with `canvas.getContext("webgl2")`; the call returns `null` if unsupported ([MDN `getContext`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLCanvasElement/getContext)). Do not maintain an equivalent Canvas-rendered 3D fallback unless accessibility or product requirements explicitly demand it; it creates two renderers to test and tune.

---

# 2. ECS in TypeScript

## Recommendation: start custom; adopt bitECS only on demonstrated need

For a practice range with one player, tens of targets, and perhaps hundreds of projectiles/effects, use a **small custom ECS-like data model**, not a broad dependency.

Use numeric entity IDs and separate data stores:

```text
World
  Transform: current + previous position / rotation
  Velocity
  Collider
  Target / HitZone
  Health
  Weapon / Cooldown
  Projectile
  Renderable
  Lifetime

Singleton resources
  InputCommand
  CameraState
  SpatialHash
  EventQueue
  RNG seed
```

Store hot numeric data in preallocated typed arrays or dense arrays keyed by entity ID. Keep system order explicit:

```text
input -> player control -> movement -> broad phase -> collision
      -> weapon/hitscan/projectile -> damage -> spawn/despawn
      -> audio/FX events -> snapshot for renderer
```

Use deferred spawn/despawn queues so a system does not mutate its query set while iterating. The renderer reads transform snapshots but does not own simulation state.

This is enough structure to separate gameplay data from behavior without making routine features depend on a generic ECS API.

## Library comparison

| Option | Pros | Cons | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Custom typed-array ECS-like world** | Smallest API, strict domain types, no dependency, easy deterministic replay/testing, ideal for known entity types | Team must implement queries, lifecycle, and storage discipline | **Best initial choice** |
| **bitECS** | TypeScript, data-oriented, flexible queries, zero dependencies, serialization, thread-friendly design; about 5 KB minified per its README | More abstract than needed for a small range; library conventions become architecture | Adopt if hot entities reach the high hundreds/thousands or composition/query needs expand |
| **ape-ecs** | Feature-rich, persisted queries, entity reference helpers, save/import support | More object/class-oriented and heavier; repository activity is older | Viable, but not the preferred fit for hot shooter simulation |
| **tiny-ecs** | Well-known minimal ECS in its own ecosystem | The commonly referenced `bakpakin/tiny-ecs` repository is **Lua**, not TypeScript | **Do not select for this TypeScript project** |

Repository evidence, reviewed 2026-07-17:

- [bitECS](https://github.com/NateTheGreatt/bitECS): TypeScript, MPL-2.0, approximately **1.46k stars**; describes itself as minimal and data-oriented.
- [ape-ecs](https://github.com/fritzy/ape-ecs): TypeScript/JavaScript, MIT, approximately **313 stars**; offers persisted queries and serialization features.
- [tiny-ecs](https://github.com/bakpakin/tiny-ecs): approximately **785 stars**, but its implementation language is Lua.

A useful escalation rule: remain custom until profiling shows either excessive entity-loop cost or feature work repeatedly requires component composition/query machinery. Switch to bitECS then, with a thin domain facade so gameplay systems do not spread library calls everywhere.

---

# 3. Fixed-timestep loop with interpolation

`requestAnimationFrame` normally follows display refresh rate, commonly 60 Hz, but should not be treated as a fixed-rate simulation clock. It pauses in most browsers when the page is backgrounded ([MDN `requestAnimationFrame`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/requestAnimationFrame)).

Run gameplay at a fixed **60 Hz** (`dt = 1 / 60`) and render every animation frame. Glenn Fiedler's fixed-timestep accumulator model avoids frame-rate-dependent physics and uses the remaining accumulator fraction as an interpolation factor ([Fix Your Timestep](https://gafferongames.com/post/fix_your_timestep/)).

```ts
const STEP_SECONDS = 1 / 60;
const MAX_FRAME_SECONDS = 0.1;
const MAX_STEPS_PER_FRAME = 5;

let previousTimeMs: number | undefined;
let accumulator = 0;

function frame(nowMs: number): void {
  requestAnimationFrame(frame);

  if (previousTimeMs === undefined) {
    previousTimeMs = nowMs;
    return;
  }

  const elapsed = Math.min(
    (nowMs - previousTimeMs) / 1000,
    MAX_FRAME_SECONDS,
  );
  previousTimeMs = nowMs;
  accumulator += elapsed;

  let steps = 0;
  while (accumulator >= STEP_SECONDS && steps < MAX_STEPS_PER_FRAME) {
    capturePreviousTransforms(world);
    simulate(world, input.drainForStep(), STEP_SECONDS);
    accumulator -= STEP_SECONDS;
    steps++;
  }

  // Deliberately drop excessive catch-up time rather than spiral forever.
  if (steps === MAX_STEPS_PER_FRAME && accumulator >= STEP_SECONDS) {
    accumulator %= STEP_SECONDS;
  }

  render(world, accumulator / STEP_SECONDS);
}
```

Rules:

- Simulation always receives exactly `1 / 60`, never an arbitrary frame delta.
- Each transform has **previous** and **current** values. Render uses `lerp(previous, current, alpha)` and quaternion slerp for rotations.
- Cap frame delta and catch-up steps. The alternative is a spiral of death under load.
- On `visibilitychange`, blur, or pause: clear held input, reset the accumulator, and reset the timestamp. The visibility event is specifically intended for reacting to tab/app backgrounding ([MDN `visibilitychange`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Document/visibilitychange_event)).
- Keep simulation deterministic where practical: seeded RNG, command input, fixed system order, and no wall-clock access from game systems.

---

# 4. Input handling

Create an `InputAdapter` that turns DOM events into an engine-owned `InputCommand`:

```text
held:      moveForward, moveBack, moveLeft, moveRight, sprint, fire
pressed:   jump, reload, interact, pause
lookDelta: accumulated mouseX/mouseY since the previous simulation tick
```

## Keyboard

- Bind gameplay controls by `KeyboardEvent.code`, not `key`. `code` is physical-key oriented and remains stable across layouts; this is specifically useful for game controls such as WASD ([MDN `KeyboardEvent.code`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/KeyboardEvent/code)).
- Track `keydown`/`keyup` state; ignore key-repeat for held actions.
- Clear all state on `blur`, pointer-lock exit, and visibility loss to prevent stuck movement.
- Only call `preventDefault()` for registered game controls while the canvas/game owns focus. Do not suppress browser shortcuts globally.
- Keep binding definitions and display labels separate; physical bindings are correct for movement, but UI should communicate a user's configured layout.

## Mouse and pointer lock

- On a deliberate “Play” canvas click/button: resume audio, request pointer lock, then enter play state.
- Listen for `pointerlockchange` and `pointerlockerror`; do not assume the request succeeded. Pointer lock requires transient user activation ([MDN `requestPointerLock`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Element/requestPointerLock)).
- While locked, accumulate `mousemove` `movementX` / `movementY`; convert them to yaw/pitch once per simulation step using sensitivity settings and a pitch clamp.
- First request `{ unadjustedMovement: true }` for raw mouse movement. If unavailable/rejected, retry normal pointer lock. This is a quality improvement, not a hard dependency; MDN documents that the option is not universally implemented ([Pointer Lock API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Pointer_Lock_API)).
- If full-screen is offered, request pointer lock before full-screen because full-screen may consume transient activation ([MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Element/requestPointerLock)).

Optional future gamepad support should produce the same `InputCommand`; poll `navigator.getGamepads()` once per simulation tick rather than letting gameplay depend on DOM gamepad event timing ([MDN Gamepad API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Gamepad_API/Using_the_Gamepad_API)).

---

# 5. Collision and spatial partitioning

Use custom collision for the range. A general rigid-body engine is unnecessary unless the game adds piles of physics props, articulated bodies, or terrain/vehicle physics.

## Narrow phase

- **Player movement:** vertical cylinder or capsule against static AABBs; sweep along intended horizontal displacement, move to time of impact, remove the normal component, and allow one or two slide iterations.
- **Static range objects:** AABBs. They are cheap because overlap uses comparisons rather than rotated-box trigonometry ([MDN 3D collision detection](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Games/Techniques/3D_collision_detection)).
- **Targets:** sphere or capsule body collider; separate small sphere/capsule hit zones for weak points if needed.
- **Hitscan weapons:** raycast the spatial hash; intersect candidate target hit zones and world blockers; select the smallest positive hit distance.
- **Physical projectiles:** test the swept segment from previous to next position, not only the final overlap. This prevents tunneling through thin targets/walls.

## Broad phase: uniform spatial hash

Build two structures:

1. **Static grid:** populated once when the range loads with walls, barriers, and target rails.
2. **Dynamic grid:** rebuilt each simulation step for moving targets and active projectile colliders.

Cell size should approximate the largest common interaction radius or target width. Insert an AABB into every overlapped cell. Queries retrieve only cells crossed by the player sweep, projectile segment, or hitscan ray. Use an integer query stamp on entities to avoid retesting an object inserted into multiple cells.

A range can begin with a flat static AABB list if it truly has only a few dozen blockers, but a uniform grid is simple enough to establish early and avoids a later broad-phase rewrite.

Do not put particle effects, decals, muzzle flashes, or other presentation-only entities into collision.

---

# 6. Assets, procedural graphics, and audio

## Visual assets

Use a hybrid pipeline:

- **Procedural/static meshes:** floor, lane markings, barriers, crates, simple targets, reticles, and repeated range dressing. Build once during loading; share geometry and materials.
- **Instancing:** repeated target stands, crates, and effects should share one geometry/material and vary by transform, color, and small per-instance parameters.
- **Atlases:** use a texture atlas for UI icons, impact decals, particles, tracer sprites, and billboard effects. Atlasing lets the renderer batch what would otherwise require texture switches ([MDN WebGL best practices](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL_API/WebGL_best_practices#batch_draw_calls)).
- **Authored 3D assets:** use `.glb`/glTF only for assets that benefit from authored animation or distinct silhouette, such as weapons, character arms, and a complex target. glTF is a transmission-oriented graphics format intended for GPU-ready scene data ([Three.js glTF guide](https://threejs.org/manual/en/load-gltf.html)).
- **Textures:** use mipmaps for 3D textures and compressed textures/KTX2 when asset size or mobile GPU memory requires it. MDN notes that mipmaps add roughly 30% texture memory but can substantially improve downscaled 3D texture sampling ([MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL_API/WebGL_best_practices#use_mipmaps_for_any_texture_youll_see_in_3d)).

Avoid generating Canvas textures or new geometries every frame. Procedural content belongs in load-time generation and reusable GPU buffers.

## Audio

- Create/resume the `AudioContext` inside the same user gesture that starts play; browsers otherwise begin it suspended ([MDN Web Audio best practices](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Audio_API/Best_practices)).
- Fetch and asynchronously decode short SFX into reusable `AudioBuffer`s with `decodeAudioData()` ([MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/BaseAudioContext/decodeAudioData)).
- Use master, music, SFX, and UI `GainNode` buses. Add a listener at the camera and simple positional panning only where it improves gameplay.
- Enforce a voice budget and priority policy: important gunshots/hits beat distant impacts; discard low-priority sounds when saturated.
- Create a fresh `AudioBufferSourceNode` for each play. Nodes are one-shot, while their `AudioBuffer` is reusable ([MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/AudioBufferSourceNode)).
- Use looping streamed music separately from short decoded gameplay SFX.

---

# 7. Performance budgets and profiling

A 60 Hz display gives 16.67 ms per frame, but browser/compositor overhead means a game should not target exactly 16.67 ms. web.dev makes the same distinction when explaining the nominal 60 FPS frame budget ([Rendering performance](https://web.dev/articles/rendering-performance)).

## Starting budgets

These are engineering alert thresholds, not universal hardware facts. Validate them on representative integrated-GPU laptops and mobile hardware.

| Area | Target p95 | Hard investigation threshold |
|---|---:|---:|
| Input + fixed simulation | <= 3 ms | > 5 ms |
| CPU render submission + HUD updates | <= 3 ms | > 5 ms |
| GPU frame | <= 7 ms | > 10 ms |
| Total frame wall time | <= 15 ms | >= 16.67 ms sustained |
| Catch-up simulation | 0-1 steps/frame normally | repeated max-step cap |
| Hot-loop allocations | none per entity/frame | visible GC pauses |
| Draw calls | start under ~100 opaque + ~50 transparent | profile before increasing |

Additional rules:

- Maintain a fixed internal render resolution policy. Cap device pixel ratio and use adaptive resolution scaling if GPU p95 exceeds budget; do not change simulation rate or gameplay FOV to recover performance.
- Prefer opaque front-to-back rendering. Keep transparent particles and alpha-blended decals bounded because they increase overdraw.
- Instance repeated meshes and batch sprite effects.
- Keep HUD DOM updates event-driven or rate-limited; never force layout from every frame.
- Do not set a VRAM budget solely from local testing. WebGL intentionally does not expose portable total GPU-memory capacity; maintain an explicit asset registry and test on target devices ([MDN WebGL best practices](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebGL_API/WebGL_best_practices)).

## Profiling toolchain

1. **In-game instrumentation:** wrap each major system and renderer phase with `performance.mark()` / `performance.measure()`. The Performance API supports custom high-resolution timeline measurements and `PerformanceObserver` ([MDN Performance API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Performance_API)).
2. **Chrome DevTools Performance panel:** record actual movement, firing, target spawning, and pause/resume flows. Repeat with CPU throttling to reveal main-thread bottlenecks; Chrome specifically recommends using throttling to simulate lower-power devices ([Chrome DevTools](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/performance)).
3. **Chrome DevTools Memory / Allocation Timeline:** find per-frame allocations and retained entities/textures. It identifies where objects were allocated and whether they remain live ([Chrome allocation profiler](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/devtools/memory-problems/allocation-profiler)).
4. **GPU timing:** where supported, use `EXT_disjoint_timer_query_webgl2` asynchronously. Read results only after query availability and discard them when `GPU_DISJOINT_EXT` is set, as demonstrated in the [Khronos extension specification](https://registry.khronos.org/webgl/extensions/EXT_disjoint_timer_query_webgl2/).
5. **Draw-call/frame inspection:** use [Spector.js](https://github.com/BabylonJS/Spector.js) to capture a WebGL frame, inspect state changes, texture binds, shader use, and draw calls. It is TypeScript/MIT and had roughly 1.6k GitHub stars when reviewed.

The first performance milestone should be a repeatable benchmark scene: maximum intended targets, sustained firing, particles, moving targets, and UI visible for 60 seconds. Track p50/p95/p99 frame time, simulation time, GPU time, draw calls, active entities, and allocation count. Optimize the measured dominant cost only.
